title: Mole Negro Oaxacan Black Mole

seo.title: Authentic Oaxacan Black Mole Recipe | Rich & Complex
seo.metaDescription: Master the rich flavors of authentic Oaxacan Black Mole with our traditional recipe. Learn to make this complex, delicious Mexican sauce from scratch for an unforgettable dinner.
longTailKeywords: how to make Oaxacan Black Mole
Oaxacan Black Mole step by step
best Oaxacan Black Mole at home
h2Headings: Pro Tips for the Best Oaxacan Black Mole
seo.imageAlt: A rich, dark bowl of authentic Oaxacan Black Mole served with chicken and rice, garnished with sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Experience the deep, complex flavors of Oaxaca with this traditional Black Mole recipe, perfect for a special dinner.

description: Mole Negro is the queen of Oaxacan cuisine, a rich and complex sauce with deep, smoky flavors. This traditional black mole combines an incredible array of chiles, spices, nuts, seeds, and chocolate, simmered to perfection. It is a labor of love that delivers an unforgettable taste of Mexico.

instructions.title: Prepare and Toast Chiles
instructions.text: Carefully wipe the dried chiles clean. Heat 1 tablespoon of lard or oil in a large skillet over medium heat. Briefly toast the chiles, one by one, for about 10-15 seconds per side until fragrant but not burnt. Immediately transfer them to a bowl of hot water and let them rehydrate for 15-20 minutes until soft.

instructions.title: Toast Aromatics and Spices
instructions.text: In the same skillet, add another tablespoon of lard or oil. Add the tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, and garlic. Cook until softened and slightly charred, about 5-7 minutes. Remove and set aside. Then, add the almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, pumpkin seeds, raisins, cinnamon stick, cloves, peppercorns, and anise seeds to the skillet. Toast for 2-3 minutes until fragrant, stirring constantly to prevent burning.

instructions.title: Fry Remaining Ingredients
instructions.text: Add the torn corn tortilla pieces and sliced plantain to the skillet. Fry until golden brown and crispy, about 3-4 minutes. Remove all toasted and fried ingredients from the skillet and combine them with the softened chiles, roasted vegetables, dried oregano, and a splash of the chile soaking water in a blender.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Paste
instructions.text: Blend all the ingredients from the previous step until a very smooth paste forms, adding hot chicken broth as needed to achieve a thick, pourable consistency. This may require blending in batches. Strain the paste through a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl, pressing firmly to extract all the liquid and discarding any solids. This step ensures a silky-smooth mole.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: Heat the remaining 1 tablespoon of lard or oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Add the strained mole paste and cook, stirring constantly, for at least 10-15 minutes. This step is crucial for developing the deep flavors of the mole and preventing it from tasting raw. The paste will darken and thicken.

instructions.title: Simmer and Finish
instructions.text: Gradually stir in the remaining hot chicken broth, a little at a time, until the mole reaches your desired consistency. It should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. Add the chopped Mexican chocolate and stir until completely melted and incorporated. Season with salt and sugar to taste. If the mole is too thick, add more broth; if too thin, simmer longer. Serve hot.

tips: Toasting chiles and spices correctly is key. Do not burn them, as this will make your mole bitter. Toast until just fragrant.
For an even richer flavor, use chilhuacle chiles if you can find them, which are traditional to Oaxacan mole. If not, the blend provided is an excellent substitute.
Mole Negro improves with age. Make it a day ahead and gently reheat it before serving to allow the complex flavors to meld further. Serve with cooked chicken or pork, rice, and warm tortillas.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the heart of Oaxaca, Mexico, where one of the world's most complex and revered sauces was born: Mole Negro, or Oaxacan Black Mole. More than just a sauce, Mole Negro is a symphony of flavors, a testament to culinary patience, and a cornerstone of Mexican gastronomic heritage, especially beloved as a traditional Mexican dinner recipe. Its profound dark color, intricate aroma, and deep, layered taste tell a story of ancient traditions, diverse ingredients, and generations of culinary mastery. If you're ready to explore a dish that promises to transform your perception of flavor, prepare to delve into the magic of Oaxacan Black Mole.

## What is Oaxacan Black Mole?

Oaxacan Black Mole, often simply called Mole Negro, is arguably the most famous and intricate of the seven moles from the Mexican state of Oaxaca. Unlike simpler sauces, a true Mole Negro is a labor of love, requiring an extensive list of ingredients—often exceeding 20 or even 30—and a multi-stage cooking process. The characteristic deep, almost obsidian black color comes from a careful balance of charred chiles, spices, and other roasted ingredients.

Its flavor profile is an extraordinary blend of smoky, sweet, spicy, earthy, and savory notes, with hints of fruit, nuts, and a subtle bitterness from Mexican chocolate. It's a sauce that evolves on the palate, revealing new dimensions with each spoonful. Historically, mole was reserved for special occasions, weddings, and communal feasts due to the sheer effort involved in its preparation. Making Oaxacan Black Mole at home is not just cooking; it's an immersive cultural experience that connects you directly to the soul of Mexico.

## Ingredients for Oaxacan Black Mole

The secret to a magnificent Oaxacan Black Mole lies in its comprehensive list of ingredients, each contributing a unique note to the final orchestra of flavor. While the exact proportions can vary by family recipe, here’s a comprehensive list of what you’ll typically need:

*   **Dried Chiles (for depth and color):**
    *   6-8 Ancho chiles (sweet, fruity, mild heat)
    *   6-8 Mulato chiles (chocolatey, smoky, mild heat)
    *   6-8 Pasilla Oaxaca chiles (smoky, medium heat)
    *   2-3 Chipotle chiles (spicy, smoky)
*   **Aromatics & Thickeners:**
    *   1 large white onion, roughly chopped
    *   6-8 cloves garlic, peeled
    *   2-3 ripe tomatoes, roasted
    *   2-3 tomatillos, husked and roasted
    *   1 plantain, sliced and fried
    *   2-3 corn tortillas, torn and fried
    *   2 slices stale bread (like bolillo or sourdough), fried
*   **Nuts & Seeds (for richness and texture):**
    *   1/2 cup almonds
    *   1/2 cup peanuts
    *   1/4 cup pumpkin seeds (pepitas)
    *   2 tablespoons sesame seeds
*   **Spices (whole, for toasting):**
    *   1 tablespoon black peppercorns
    *   1 teaspoon cumin seeds
    *   1/2 teaspoon whole cloves
    *   1 stick cinnamon (Mexican canela preferred)
    *   1/2 teaspoon anise seeds
    *   1/2 teaspoon dried thyme
    *   1/2 teaspoon dried marjoram
    *   1/2 teaspoon dried oregano
*   **Sweeteners & Fruits:**
    *   1/4 cup raisins
    *   1/4 cup pitted prunes
    *   1-2 ounces piloncillo (Mexican brown sugar cone) or dark brown sugar
*   **Flavor Enhancers:**
    *   1-2 tablets (90g) authentic Mexican chocolate (e.g., Ibarra or Mayordomo), unsweetened or semi-sweet
    *   4-6 cups chicken or turkey broth (or vegetable broth for a vegetarian option)
    *   1/4 cup lard or vegetable oil (for frying the paste)
    *   Salt to taste

## Step-by-Step Oaxacan Black Mole Instructions

Making Oaxacan Black Mole is a process, not a sprint. Take your time, and savor each step.

1.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Carefully toast the dried chiles in a dry skillet over medium heat for 30-60 seconds per side until aromatic and slightly puffed. Be careful not to burn them, as this will make the mole bitter. Stem and deseed the chiles. Place them in a bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for at least 30 minutes until soft.
2.  **Roast and Fry:**
    *   **Roast:** On a baking sheet, roast the tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, and garlic until softened and slightly charred.
    *   **Fry:** In a skillet with a little lard or oil, fry the plantain slices, torn tortillas, and bread until golden. Remove and set aside.
    *   **Toast Nuts & Seeds:** In the same skillet (or a dry one), toast the almonds, peanuts, pumpkin seeds, and sesame seeds until fragrant. Be careful not to burn the sesame seeds.
    *   **Toast Spices:** Briefly toast the whole peppercorns, cumin seeds, cloves, cinnamon stick, and anise seeds until aromatic.
3.  **Blend the Ingredients:** Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving some of the soaking liquid. In a powerful blender, combine the chiles, roasted vegetables, fried plantain/tortillas/bread, toasted nuts, seeds, spices, raisins, and prunes. Add 1-2 cups of broth (or chile soaking liquid) as needed to create a thick, smooth paste. You may need to do this in batches. Blend until absolutely smooth, adding more liquid if necessary. This step is crucial for the mole's texture.
4.  **Fry the Paste:** Heat the 1/4 cup of lard or oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour in the blended mole paste. This step is vital for developing the deep color and rich flavor. Stir constantly, scraping the bottom, for 20-30 minutes, or until the paste darkens significantly and thickens, reducing in volume. It should look very dark and glossy.
5.  **Simmer the Mole:** Gradually stir in the remaining chicken or turkey broth, 1 cup at a time, until the mole reaches your desired consistency (it should be thick but pourable). Add the piloncillo (or brown sugar) and Mexican chocolate. Bring to a gentle simmer, then reduce heat to low, cover, and cook for at least 1 to 2 hours, stirring occasionally. This long simmer allows all the complex flavors to meld and deepen.
6.  **Adjust Seasoning:** Taste the Oaxacan Black Mole and adjust salt, sweetness, or spice as needed. If it's too thick, add a little more broth. If it's too thin, simmer uncovered for a bit longer. For an ultra-smooth mole, you can pass it through a fine-mesh sieve, though many prefer the rustic texture.

## Pro Tips for the Best Oaxacan Black Mole

*   **Quality Ingredients are Key:** The depth of flavor in Oaxacan Black Mole comes from the quality of each component. Seek out good quality dried chiles and authentic Mexican chocolate.
*   **Patience is a Virtue:** This is not a quick meal. Embrace the multi-stage process, as each step contributes to the final masterpiece. Don't rush the toasting, roasting, or simmering.
*   **Don't Burn the Chiles:** When toasting chiles, a slight char is good, but burning will introduce an unpleasant bitterness. Work quickly and in a well-ventilated area.
*   **Blend Thoroughly:** A powerful blender is your best friend. A smooth paste is essential for a velvety mole. If your blender isn't super strong, you might need to strain the mole later.
*   **The Fry is Critical:** Frying the mole paste in lard or oil is non-negotiable. This step toasts the flavors further, deepens the color, and creates the characteristic richness.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** Mole is a highly personal dish. Continuously taste and adjust the balance of sweet, spicy, and savory.
*   **Make Ahead:** Mole tastes even better the next day! The flavors continue to meld and deepen overnight. It also freezes beautifully.

## Serving Suggestions for Mole Negro

Oaxacan Black Mole is most famously served with poultry, typically chicken or turkey. Here are some classic and creative ways to enjoy this incredible sauce:

*   **With Chicken or Turkey:** Simmer cooked chicken pieces (thighs, legs, breasts) directly in the mole sauce until heated through and coated.
*   **Rice and Tortillas:** Always serve with fluffy white rice to soak up every drop of the rich sauce, and warm corn tortillas for scooping.
*   **Enmoladas:** Use the mole as a luscious sauce for enchiladas, often filled with shredded chicken or cheese.
*   **Tamales:** A generous dollop of Oaxacan Black Mole is a divine accompaniment to savory tamales.
*   **Vegetarian Options:** Mole Negro is fantastic over roasted sweet potatoes, plantains, mushrooms, or even hearty black bean patties.
*   **Garnishes:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, fresh cilantro, thinly sliced red onion, or crumbled queso fresco adds both beauty and additional flavor.

## Variations and Substitutions

While the traditional Oaxacan Black Mole recipe is cherished for its authenticity, you can adapt it based on availability or dietary preferences:

*   **Chiles:** If you can't find all the specific chiles, you can adjust the ratios. Ancho, mulato, and pasilla chiles are the most crucial for the dark color and flavor profile. You might substitute some guajillo chiles for a milder, fruitier note, but be aware it will affect the final color.
*   **Nuts & Seeds:** If you have allergies, you can omit certain nuts. Cashews or pecans can be used as alternatives to almonds or peanuts.
*   **Sweeteners:** Dark brown sugar can replace piloncillo. A small amount of honey or agave can also be used, but adjust to taste.
*   **Chocolate:** While Mexican chocolate is ideal for its specific blend of cacao, sugar, and cinnamon, a high-quality unsweetened or dark chocolate (70% cacao or higher) can be used. Add a pinch of cinnamon if using regular dark chocolate.
*   **Broth:** Chicken broth is traditional, but turkey or even rich vegetable broth works beautifully, especially for vegetarian versions.
*   **Vegetarian/Vegan:** Use vegetable broth instead of chicken broth, and substitute vegetable oil for lard. Ensure any bread used is vegan.

Crafting Oaxacan Black Mole is a culinary adventure that rewards patience with unparalleled flavor. This iconic Mexican dinner recipe isn't just a meal; it's a celebration of heritage, a deep dive into complex flavors, and a truly unforgettable experience for anyone who tastes it. So gather your ingredients, clear your schedule, and prepare to create a culinary masterpiece that will transport your taste buds straight to the vibrant kitchens of Oaxaca.
